title: Baghali polo
subtitle: Persian dill and broad bean rice
excerpt: Basmati rice layered with a generous quantity of fresh or frozen broad beans and an even more generous quantity of dill, steamed together with butter until fragrant and fluffy with a golden tahdig crust. Served alongside slow-braised lamb shank. The combination of dill, broad bean, and saffron is one of the most celebrated in Persian cooking.
sourceNotes: Baghali polo (broad bean and dill rice) is one of the classic Persian mixed rice dishes, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). It is traditionally served at Persian New Year and at celebrations, alongside braised lamb shank (mahicheh). The dish is characterised by an unusually large quantity of dill, which perfumes the entire pot.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare the beans and double-pod them ahead. Cook the rice fresh on the day.

content.text: The dill is used in quantities that feel excessive — 100 g of fresh dill for 4 servings. This is correct. The dill is the flavour of the dish, not an accent. It is mixed through the parboiled rice at the layering stage, distributed throughout the pot so every forkful has dill in it. Fresh dill is essential; dried dill will not produce the same result. Frozen broad beans work well, especially the smaller ones that do not need double-podding.

The broad beans should be double-podded to remove the outer pale green skin and reveal the bright inner bean. This step takes time but is worth it: the inner bean is tender and sweet, while the outer skin is tough and slightly bitter. Frozen baby broad beans are usually small enough to use whole without double-podding.

items.prepNote: rinsed and soaked in salted water for 1 hour

content.text: Drain the {{basmati-rice}} and parboil in a large pot of well-salted boiling water for 5 to 6 minutes until just cooked on the outside but firm in the centre. In the last 2 minutes of parboiling, add the double-podded {{broad-beans}} to the pot. Drain both together. Gently mix the {{dill}} through the rice and beans.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large non-stick pot over medium-high heat with 2 tablespoons of water. Mound the rice, bean, and dill mixture into the pot. Poke 4 to 5 holes through with a spoon handle. Wrap the lid in a tea towel and cover. Cook on medium-high for 5 minutes, then reduce to the lowest heat for 30 to 35 minutes. Remove from the heat, rest for 5 minutes. Spoon the {{saffron}} water over the top layer before serving. Scoop out onto a platter.

items.symptom: Broad beans are tough and chewy
items.cause: Not double-podded or outer skin not removed
items.fix: Double-podding removes the tough outer skin to reveal the tender inner bean. The beans should be bright green and yielding, not pale and firm

items.cause: Heat too high or cooked too long after the steam stage
items.fix: The dill turns grey with extended exposure to high heat. Reduce the heat as soon as the steam stage begins. Serve immediately after the rest period

items.cause: Too little butter or broad beans added too early and the starch muddied the water
items.fix: Use the full amount of butter — it carries the flavour and creates the steam. Add beans only in the last 2 minutes of parboiling to prevent starch from clouding the water

items.cause: Non-stick pan not hot enough or butter too little
items.fix: The initial blast of medium-high heat is what sets the crust. Increase the butter to 70 g and the water to 3 tablespoons for a more reliable crust

content.text: Baghali polo is one of the most festive dishes in Iranian cooking, reserved for occasions and celebrations, particularly Persian New Year in March when the broad beans are at their seasonal best. The dish is almost always served alongside mahicheh (slow-braised lamb shank), whose gelatinous cooking juices are spooned over the rice at the table. The combination is one of the great pairings of Persian cuisine: the grassy freshness of the dill and the sweetness of the broad beans against the richness of the lamb. In spring in Iran, baghali polo with lamb shank is as much a seasonal event as a dish.
